sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A genetic counselor evaluated a family with a rare autosomal recessive metabolic disorder. Two unaffected parents had an affected child. DNA testing showed that both parents carried one pathogenic allele in Gene Q. The affected child had two pathogenic alleles. A second pregnancy was being discussed. The counselor explained that each pregnancy is an independent event and that carrier parents have a predictable Mendelian risk for an affected child.
questions.stem: What is the probability that two carrier parents will have an affected child?

explanations.D: Correct. For an autosomal recessive condition, two heterozygous carrier parents have a 1 in 4 chance of an affected homozygous recessive child each pregnancy.
explanations.A: Carrier parents can have affected children, so the risk is not 0%.
explanations.B: Fifty percent is the probability of a carrier child, not an affected child.
explanations.C: Not every child will inherit both pathogenic alleles.

questions.stem: Why can both parents be unaffected?

choices.A: One functional allele is sufficient to prevent the recessive phenotype
choices.C: The pathogenic allele is dominant in carriers
choices.D: The parents have no Gene Q alleles

explanations.A: Correct. Carriers have one normal allele and one pathogenic allele; for recessive disorders, one functional copy often prevents disease.
explanations.B: The pedigree pattern described is autosomal recessive, not mitochondrial.
explanations.C: If the allele were dominant, carriers would typically be affected.
explanations.D: The DNA testing showed both parents have Gene Q alleles.

questions.stem: What is the probability that an unaffected sibling of the affected child is a carrier?

explanations.C: Correct. Among unaffected children from two carrier parents, the possible genotypes are AA, Aa, and aA. Two of these three unaffected genotypes are carriers, so the conditional probability is 2/3.
explanations.A: 1/4 is the probability of being affected before conditioning on unaffected status.
explanations.B: 1/2 ignores the conditional genotype distribution among unaffected siblings.
explanations.D: 3/4 is the probability of being unaffected, not carrier given unaffected.

questions.stem: What does it mean that each pregnancy is an independent event?

choices.B: The risk remains 25% regardless of previous affected or unaffected children
choices.A: After one affected child, the next child must be unaffected
choices.C: Risk doubles after an affected child
choices.D: The parents stop being carriers after one pregnancy

explanations.B: Correct. Independent Mendelian assortment means the genotype outcome of one pregnancy does not change the allele segregation probabilities for the next.
explanations.A: Previous outcome does not force the next outcome.
explanations.C: The Mendelian risk does not double because of prior events.
explanations.D: Parental genotypes do not change after a pregnancy.

questions.stem: Which inheritance pattern is most consistent with unaffected parents having an affected child of either sex?

explanations.A: Correct. Autosomal recessive inheritance commonly produces affected children from unaffected carrier parents and can affect either sex.
explanations.B: Complete-penetrance autosomal dominant disease usually has an affected parent.
explanations.C: Y-linked inheritance affects only genetic males and requires paternal transmission.
explanations.D: X-linked dominant inheritance would not fit the described autosomal carrier pattern.

questions.passage: In a large randomly mating population, a recessive disease affects 1 in 10,000 individuals. Researchers assumed Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium for a first approximation. The disease allele is represented as q, and affected individuals are q². The population was screened to estimate carrier frequency for genetic counseling. The assumptions included no strong selection, mutation, migration, nonrandom mating, or genetic drift.

explanations.D: Correct. q is the square root of q². The square root of 0.0001 is 0.01.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Estimated affected frequency, recessive allele frequency, and carrier frequency under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium.

questions.stem: What is the approximate carrier frequency?

explanations.C: Correct. For a rare allele, p is close to 1, so 2pq ≈ 2(1)(0.01)=0.02, or about 2%.
explanations.A: 0.01% corresponds to the affected frequency q², not carriers.
explanations.B: 25% is far too high for q=0.01.
explanations.D: 98% is approximately neither carrier nor affected frequency.

questions.stem: Which assumption is required for Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?

choices.B: Random mating with no major evolutionary forces

explanations.B: Correct. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium assumes random mating and no strong mutation, migration, selection, drift, or other forces changing allele frequencies.

questions.stem: Why is carrier frequency much higher than affected frequency for a rare recessive allele?

choices.D: Heterozygotes are proportional to 2pq, whereas affected individuals are q²
choices.A: Carriers must have two recessive alleles
choices.B: Affected individuals are counted as p²
choices.C: Dominant alleles cannot exist in the population

explanations.D: Correct. When q is small, q² is much smaller than 2q. Thus heterozygous carriers can be far more common than affected homozygotes.
explanations.A: Carriers are heterozygotes with one recessive allele.
explanations.B: Affected recessive individuals are q², not p².
explanations.C: Dominant and recessive alleles can coexist.

questions.stem: Which event would most likely violate Hardy-Weinberg assumptions?

choices.A: A small isolated population undergoes genetic drift

explanations.A: Correct. Genetic drift is a random change in allele frequency, especially in small populations, and violates Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium.
explanations.B: Large population size supports the assumptions.
explanations.C: Random mating is an assumption, not a violation.
explanations.D: Constant allele frequencies are consistent with equilibrium.
